pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi prescribes a five-step skill-learning method: (1) produce high volume, (2) isolate the top 10% of outputs, (3) identify the differences between the top 10% and the bottom 90%, (4) determine which difference is the causal variable, (5) eliminate the mistakes the bottom 90% keeps making and replicate the top 10%, then repeat 100 more times.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the specificity of instruction required is inversely correlated with the skill level of the recipient: a low-skill person needs every sub-step explained (e.g., what a browser is, how to type), while a high-skill person can execute from a single high-level directive. Entrepreneurs misidentify this as a motivation problem when it is actually a skill-gap problem.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that regret is generated by imagining the upside of an unchosen path without accounting for the downside (sacrifice, cost, risk) of that path; this asymmetric mental accounting makes unchosen alternatives appear more attractive than they actually were.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the time and difficulty required to succeed at uncertain bets is both faster and less severe than people anticipate before starting. pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi distinguishes three social-signal terms: shame (breaking someone else's rules), guilt (breaking your own rules), and cringe (supposed secondhand embarrassment from an observer).
